title: Can you social engineer an AI? Plus: AI worms and the nonhuman identity problem
summary: IBM Security Intelligence podcast discussing social engineering attacks on AI systems, self-replicating AI worms, and nonhuman identity management. Speakers examine whether AI agents are more susceptible to manipulation than humans and debate the severity of emerging AI security threats.
writeup: IBM's Security Intelligence podcast convenes three X-Force practitioners to dissect a real-world case in which attackers tricked Meta's AI customer support agent into updating Instagram account recovery emails to attacker-controlled addresses, effectively handing over account access without any secondary authentication. The panel traces the root cause to a fundamental property of current AI agents: they optimise for helpfulness as instructed but carry no embedded common sense about which requests are unreasonable, making them more susceptible to social engineering than a trained human operator. The through-line is that deploying agents in high-stakes customer-facing roles requires explicit, enumerated negative constraints, not just a general directive to assist.
key_points: Attackers posed as legitimate Instagram account owners, contacted Meta's AI customer support agent via message, and requested an email address change to one they controlled; the agent complied without requiring additional authentication, allowing password recovery and full account takeover (reported by 404 Media).

The attack required only a VPN to spoof the victim's geographic location and a plain-language request: because the agent's system prompt said to be a helpful customer support assistant, it executed the request rather than questioning the absence of secondary verification.

Jeff Crume (IBM distinguished engineer) frames the core vulnerability as AI having a thousand PhDs but no lived experience above ground: high book intelligence with no street sense, meaning agents cannot yet recognize when a request is inherently unreasonable without being explicitly told so.

The lesson the panel draws is that general helpfulness directives are insufficient; builders must enumerate specific prohibitions (for example, do not change account recovery email without multi-factor verification) because an agent will otherwise default to satisfying any plausible-sounding request.

Claire Nunez (IBM X-Force Cyber Range) notes that agents lack nuance and will not spontaneously ask why a user wants to change an email, a question a cautious human agent would ask, so safeguards must come from the system design rather than the model's judgment.
